Do not cheat by using online solutions or hints specific to this task.

Resource limits: CPUs: 1, memory: 4096 MB, storage: 10240 MB.
Solve this task in 86400 seconds (24 hours).

I'll start by examining the data files to understand the task structure.

bb4c434,"People tend to use there cars so much, they basically can't live or get through the day with out their car. We use the car to go every where rather it's to the grocery store , work or just to go hang out with friends and family! Just traveling in general. Well imagine if we limited the usage of cars in our community! I strongly believe that doing this would have many advantages to our community for many reasons like it will make people less stressful all the time, there would be less accidents and it would definitly be better for the environment.

OMG I ran out of gas or OH my i have to take my car to the shop to get it fixed is usually the two main things you hear people stay when the word ""car"" comes up. Having a car comes with alot of stress because it of expenses and having to take of its every need and usually when someone is stressed they are not the best person you wanna have a conversation with. I think that if we limit the usage of cars a lot of people in the community will be alot happier. According to the article

""Car-free Cities""

by

Elisabeth Rosenthal in source 2 a lady name Heidrun Walter says "" When i had a car i was always tense. I'm much happier this way."" and i think that is a perfect example of limited car usauage can make a person happier.

In addition, I feel that if that if we limit the car usage in our community it will make this community a much safer environment. We hear and see about many car accidents in our country and community and cars can be very very dangerous. People often get injuried and/or die in car recks to. So limitting the car usage in the communitry will keep people more safe and most importantly alive . Kids will be able to run aroumd the neighborhood and play with their friends and also cross the street safely without the disruption of a car coming and stopping them from what they are doing  because they do not wants to get hurt from the car coming because they do not know who is driving the car.

Last but not least limiting the use of car usage in the community because it would definitly be better for the environment. In science class we often talk about how cars polute the air and just mess up the waehter in the car and me my self i can also feel the car changing the more i see cars more cars in the streets. I think that it makes the days hotter and i also think that it makes the winters come around earlier and it makes it alot more colder. I live in the Sunshine florida and in the south. So all year round its usually hot extremly hot but has i realize more people are driving around the winers seem ALOT more cold then it usually is and the cold last for longer periods of time.

In conclusion, I belive that if we limit the car usuage in our community it will make it a better place for many reasons. Like it will make people less stressful and  be happier, there will be less accidents on the road and it will also help the environment a lot.                        "
44e88b0,"Imagine being a top scientist at NASA and Viking 1 spacecraft ""spotted the shadowy likeness of a human face."" A lot of people thought of alien life possibility, but others thought it to be just a natural landform. What would your thoughts be about the image? The face of Mars is a natural landform. There's similar landforms on Earth, high pixel photos taken, and not enough reliable information to support the alien life theory.

To begin, there are similar landforms on Earth to the one on Mars. As stated in the text, ""what the picture actually shows is the Martian equivalent of a butte or mesa- landforms common around the American West."" Also stated in the text is Garvin giving his opinion, ""it reminds me most of Middle Butte in the Snake River Plain of Idaho.""



## Overview

### Description

Essay writing is a critical method for assessing student learning and performance, yet manually grading essays is time-consuming for educators. Automated Writing Evaluation (AWE) systems aim to supplement this effort by scoring essays and providing timely feedback to students. However, these systems often come with high costs, limiting their accessibility to broader educational communities. This competition seeks to advance automated essay scoring by leveraging a comprehensive dataset that includes diverse samples aligned with current educational standards.

### Context

The first automated essay scoring competition, hosted twelve years ago, marked a significant milestone in exploring machine learning's role in grading student-written essays. This updated competition aims to build upon past achievements, offering a dataset enriched with high-quality, realistic classroom writing samples. By incorporating economic and geographic diversity, the dataset aims to mitigate potential algorithmic biases present in earlier efforts.

### Goals

Participants in this competition are tasked with developing open-source algorithms for essay scoring, improving upon previous initiatives like the Automated Student Assessment Prize (ASAP) competition of 2012. Hosted by Vanderbilt University in partnership with The Learning Agency Lab, this initiative aims to democratize access to robust AWE solutions. Winning solutions will be released as open source, ensuring widespread availability and support for educators, particularly in underserved districts.

### Evaluation

Submissions will be evaluated based on the quadratic weighted kappa, measuring agreement between predicted and actual essay scores. This metric ranges from 0 (random agreement) to 1 (perfect agreement), with negative values indicating less agreement than expected by chance.

### Submission File

For each essay_id in the test set, participants are required to predict the corresponding score. The submission file should follow this format:

```
essay_id,score
000d118,3
000fe60,3
001ab80,4
...
```

### Acknowledgments

Vanderbilt University and The Learning Agency Lab acknowledge the support of the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation, Schmidt Futures, and Chan Zuckerberg Initiative for their contributions to this initiative.

steps.text:
Now I understand the data:
- Scores range from 1-6
- Training data: 14,710 essays
- Test data: 23,136 essays
- Essays are fairly long (mean ~2000 chars)

I need to build a model that predicts essay scores (1-6) based on the text. The evaluation metric is quadratic weighted kappa.

Given the resource constraints (1 CPU, 4GB memory), I should be careful about what approach to use. Let me think about options:

1. **Feature-based approach**: Extract text features (length, word count, vocabulary richness, grammar features, etc.) and use a traditional ML model like LightGBM or Random Forest.
2. **Transformer-based approach**: Fine-tune a small transformer model. This might be too heavy for 4GB memory.
3. **TF-IDF + ML**: Use TF-IDF features with a classifier.

Given the constraints, I'll go with a hybrid approach:
1. Extract meaningful text features
2. Use TF-IDF for text representation
3. Train a model like LightGBM or Ridge regression

Let me check what packages are available.

1. Text feature engineering (word count, sentence count, vocabulary richness, spelling errors, etc.)
2. TF-IDF features
3. XGBoost or sklearn ensemble model

Let me create a comprehensive solution. I'll use:
- Hand-crafted text features (length, word count, sentence count, avg word length, unique words ratio, punctuation counts, etc.)
- TF-IDF with truncated SVD for dimensionality reduction
- XGBoost regressor with rounding for final predictions

Let me write the solution script.

steps.text:
Let me write a comprehensive Python script that:
1. Extracts text features
2. Builds TF-IDF features
3. Trains an XGBoost model
4. Generates predictions

I need to be careful with memory since we have 4GB limit and ~38K essays total.

toolCalls.name: write_file
toolCalls.args:
{"file_path": "/app/solve.py", "content": "import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import re
import string
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer
from sklearn.decomposition import TruncatedSVD
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.model_selection import StratifiedKFold
from sklearn.metrics import cohen_kappa_score
from scipy.sparse import hstack, csr_matrix
import xgboost as xgb
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

# ============================================================
# Feature Engineering
# ============================================================

def extract_text_features(texts):
    """Extract hand-crafted features from essay texts."""
    features = []
    for text in texts:
        if not isinstance(text, str):
            text = ""
        
        f = {}
        
        # Basic length features
        f['char_count'] = len(text)
        f['char_no_space'] = len(text.replace(' ', ''))
        
        # Word-level features
        words = text.split()
        f['word_count'] = len(words)
        f['unique_word_count'] = len(set(w.lower() for w in words))
        f['lexical_diversity'] = f['unique_word_count'] / max(f['word_count'], 1)
        
        # Average word length
        if words:
            f['avg_word_length'] = np.mean([len(w) for w in words])
            f['std_word_length'] = np.std([len(w) for w in words])
        else:
            f['avg_word_length'] = 0
            f['std_word_length'] = 0
        
        # Sentence features
        sentences = re.split(r'[.!?]+', text)
        sentences = [s.strip() for s in sentences if s.strip()]
        f['sentence_count'] = len(sentences)
        f['avg_sentence_length'] = f['word_count'] / max(f['sentence_count'], 1)
        
        # Paragraph features
        paragraphs = text.split('\n')
        paragraphs = [p.strip() for p in paragraphs if p.strip()]
        f['paragraph_count'] = len(paragraphs)
        f['avg_paragraph_length'] = f['word_count'] / max(f['paragraph_count'], 1)
        
        # Punctuation features
        f['comma_count'] = text.count(',')
        f['semicolon_count'] = text.count(';')
        f['colon_count'] = text.count(':')
        f['exclamation_count'] = text.count('!')
        f['question_count'] = text.count('?')
        f['period_count'] = text.count('.')
        f['quote_count'] = text.count('"') + text.count("'")
        f['dash_count'] = text.count('-') + text.count('—')
        f['parenthesis_count'] = text.count('(') + text.count(')')
        f['total_punctuation'] = sum(text.count(p) for p in string.punctuation)
        f['punct_ratio'] = f['total_punctuation'] / max(f['char_count'], 1)
        
        # Capitalization features
        f['uppercase_count'] = sum(1 for c in text if c.isupper())
        f['uppercase_ratio'] = f['uppercase_count'] / max(f['char_no_space'], 1)
        
        # Long words (sophistication indicator)
        long_words = [w for w in words if len(w) >= 7]
        f['long_word_count'] = len(long_words)
        f['long_word_ratio'] = len(long_words) / max(f['word_count'], 1)
        
        # Very long words (8+ chars)
        very_long_words = [w for w in words if len(w) >= 9]
        f['very_long_word_count'] = len(very_long_words)
        f['very_long_word_ratio'] = len(very_long_words) / max(f['word_count'], 1)
        
        # Spelling/grammar error proxies
        # Count words that are all caps (potential shouting/errors)
        all_caps = [w for w in words if w.isupper() and len(w) > 1]
        f['all_caps_count'] = len(all_caps)
        
        # Count repeated characters (e.g., "soooo")
        repeated_chars = len(re.findall(r'(.)\1{3,}', text))
        f['repeated_chars'] = repeated_chars
        
        # Count common misspelling patterns
        # Words with numbers mixed in
        mixed_alphanum = len(re.findall(r'\b[a-zA-Z]*\d[a-zA-Z]*\b', text))
        f['mixed_alphanum'] = mixed_alphanum
        
        # Transition/discourse markers (essay quality indicator)
        discourse_markers = [
            'however', 'therefore', 'furthermore', 'moreover', 'consequently',
            'nevertheless', 'additionally', 'similarly', 'conversely',
            'in conclusion', 'in addition', 'for example', 'for instance',
            'on the other hand', 'in contrast', 'as a result',
            'in summary', 'to begin', 'first of all', 'secondly',
            'finally', 'lastly', 'in other words', 'specifically',
            'particularly', 'indeed', 'certainly', 'undoubtedly',
            'although', 'despite', 'whereas', 'while', 'meanwhile',
            'subsequently', 'thereafter', 'hence', 'thus', 'accordingly'
        ]
        text_lower = text.lower()
        f['discourse_marker_count'] = sum(1 for m in discourse_markers if m in text_lower)
        f['discourse_marker_density'] = f['discourse_marker_count'] / max(f['word_count'], 1) * 100
        
        # Quote usage (referencing sources)
        quote_patterns = len(re.findall(r'"[^"]{10,}"', text))
        f['quote_usage'] = quote_patterns
        
        # Vocabulary sophistication - count of academic-sounding words
        academic_words = [
            'analyze', 'analysis', 'significant', 'evidence', 'demonstrate',
            'illustrate', 'perspective', 'argument', 'conclusion', 'essential',
            'fundamental', 'contribute', 'establish', 'implement', 'substantial',
            'comprehensive', 'elaborate', 'emphasize', 'evaluate', 'examine',
            'indicate', 'interpret', 'justify', 'maintain', 'obtain',
            'perceive', 'relevant', 'represent', 'require', 'resolve',
            'assert', 'claim', 'contend', 'imply', 'suggest',
            'acknowledge', 'address', 'advocate', 'challenge', 'consider'
        ]
        f['academic_word_count'] = sum(1 for w in academic_words if w in text_lower)
        f['academic_word_density'] = f['academic_word_count'] / max(f['word_count'], 1) * 100
        
        # Type-token ratio for different segments
        if f['word_count'] > 0:
            first_half = words[:len(words)//2]
            second_half = words[len(words)//2:]
            f['ttr_first_half'] = len(set(w.lower() for w in first_half)) / max(len(first_half), 1)
            f['ttr_second_half'] = len(set(w.lower() for w in second_half)) / max(len(second_half), 1)
        else:
            f['ttr_first_half'] = 0
            f['ttr_second_half'] = 0
        
        # Newline patterns
        f['newline_count'] = text.count('\n')
        f['double_newline_count'] = text.count('\n\n')
        
        # Spaces
        f['space_count'] = text.count(' ')
        f['spaces_per_word'] = f['space_count'] / max(f['word_count'], 1)
        
        features.append(f)
    
    return pd.DataFrame(features)


# ============================================================
# Load Data
# ============================================================
print("Loading data...")
train_df = pd.read_csv('/app/train.csv')
test_df = pd.read_csv('/app/test.csv')

print(f"Train: {train_df.shape}, Test: {test_df.shape}")
print(f"Score distribution:\n{train_df['score'].value_counts().sort_index()}")

# ============================================================
# Extract Features
# ============================================================
print("\nExtracting text features...")
train_text_features = extract_text_features(train_df['full_text'].values)
test_text_features = extract_text_features(test_df['full_text'].values)

text_feature_cols = train_text_features.columns.tolist()
print(f"Text features: {len(text_feature_cols)}")

# ============================================================
# TF-IDF Features
# ============================================================
print("\nBuilding TF-IDF features...")
# Combine train and test for consistent vocabulary
all_texts = pd.concat([train_df['full_text'], test_df['full_text']], ignore_index=True)

tfidf = TfidfVectorizer(
    max_features=10000,
    ngram_range=(1, 2),
    min_df=3,
    max_df=0.95,
    sublinear_tf=True,
    strip_accents='unicode',
    token_pattern=r'(?u)\b\w\w+\b'
)

print("Fitting TF-IDF...")
tfidf_all = tfidf.fit_transform(all_texts)
tfidf_train = tfidf_all[:len(train_df)]
tfidf_test = tfidf_all[len(train_df):]

print(f"TF-IDF shape: {tfidf_train.shape}")

# SVD for dimensionality reduction
print("Applying TruncatedSVD...")
n_components = 150
svd = TruncatedSVD(n_components=n_components, random_state=42)
svd_train = svd.fit_transform(tfidf_train)
svd_test = svd.transform(tfidf_test)

print(f"Explained variance ratio: {svd.explained_variance_ratio_.sum():.4f}")

# ============================================================
# Combine Features
# ============================================================
print("\nCombining features...")
# Scale text features
scaler = StandardScaler()
train_text_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(train_text_features.values)
test_text_scaled = scaler.transform(test_text_features.values)

# Scale SVD features
svd_scaler = StandardScaler()
svd_train_scaled = svd_scaler.fit_transform(svd_train)
svd_test_scaled = svd_scaler.transform(svd_test)

# Combine: text features + SVD features
X_train = np.hstack([train_text_scaled, svd_train_scaled])
X_test = np.hstack([test_text_scaled, svd_test_scaled])
y_train = train_df['score'].values

print(f"Final feature matrix: {X_train.shape}")

# ============================================================
# Train Model with Cross-Validation
# ============================================================
print("\nTraining XGBoost model...")

# XGBoost parameters
xgb_params = {
    'objective': 'reg:squarederror',
    'eval_metric': 'rmse',
    'max_depth': 6,
    'learning_rate': 0.05,
    'n_estimators': 500,
    'subsample': 0.8,
    'colsample_bytree': 0.8,
    'min_child_weight': 5,
    'reg_alpha': 1.0,
    'reg_lambda': 1.0,
    'random_state': 42,
    'n_jobs': 1,
    'verbosity': 0
}

# Cross-validation
n_splits = 5
skf = StratifiedKFold(n_splits=n_splits, shuffle=True, random_state=42)
oof_preds = np.zeros(len(train_df))
fold_kappas = []

for fold, (train_idx, val_idx) in enumerate(skf.split(X_train, y_train)):
    X_tr, X_val = X_train[train_idx], X_train[val_idx]
    y_tr, y_val = y_train[train_idx], y_train[val_idx]
    
    model = xgb.XGBRegressor(**xgb_params)
    model.fit(
        X_tr, y_tr,
        eval_set=[(X_val, y_val)],
        verbose=False
    )
    
    val_preds = model.predict(X_val)
    oof_preds[val_idx] = val_preds
    
    # Round predictions for kappa calculation
    val_preds_rounded = np.clip(np.round(val_preds), 1, 6).astype(int)
    kappa = cohen_kappa_score(y_val, val_preds_rounded, weights='quadratic')
    fold_kappas.append(kappa)
    print(f"  Fold {fold+1}: QWK = {kappa:.4f}")

# Overall OOF score
oof_rounded = np.clip(np.round(oof_preds), 1, 6).astype(int)
overall_kappa = cohen_kappa_score(y_train, oof_rounded, weights='quadratic')
print(f"\nOverall OOF QWK: {overall_kappa:.4f}")
print(f"Mean fold QWK: {np.mean(fold_kappas):.4f} +/- {np.std(fold_kappas):.4f}")

# ============================================================
# Train Final Model and Predict
# ============================================================
print("\nTraining final model on full training data...")
final_model = xgb.XGBRegressor(**xgb_params)
final_model.fit(X_train, y_train, verbose=False)

test_preds = final_model.predict(X_test)
test_preds_rounded = np.clip(np.round(test_preds), 1, 6).astype(int)

print(f"Prediction distribution:\n{pd.Series(test_preds_rounded).value_counts().sort_index()}")

# ============================================================
# Save Submission
# ============================================================
import os
os.makedirs('/app/output', exist_ok=True)

submission = pd.DataFrame({
    'essay_id': test_df['essay_id'],
    'score': test_preds_rounded
})
submission.to_csv('/app/output/submission.csv', index=False)
print(f"\nSubmission saved to /app/output/submission.csv")
print(f"Submission shape: {submission.shape}")
print(submission.head(10))
"}

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import re
import string
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer
from sklearn.decomposition import TruncatedSVD
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.model_selection import StratifiedKFold
from sklearn.metrics import cohen_kappa_score
from scipy.sparse import hstack, csr_matrix
import xgboost as xgb
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

# ============================================================
# Feature Engineering
# ============================================================

def extract_text_features(texts):
    """Extract hand-crafted features from essay texts."""
    features = []
    for text in texts:
        if not isinstance(text, str):
            text = ""
        
        f = {}
        
        # Basic length features
        f['char_count'] = len(text)
        f['char_no_space'] = len(text.replace(' ', ''))
        
        # Word-level features
        words = text.split()
        f['word_count'] = len(words)
        f['unique_word_count'] = len(set(w.lower() for w in words))
        f['lexical_diversity'] = f['unique_word_count'] / max(f['word_count'], 1)
        
        # Average word length
        if words:
            f['avg_word_length'] = np.mean([len(w) for w in words])
            f['std_word_length'] = np.std([len(w) for w in words])
        else:
            f['avg_word_length'] = 0
            f['std_word_length'] = 0

**Model summary:**
- **Features:** 42 hand-crafted text features (word count, sentence length, vocabulary diversity, discourse markers, punctuation, academic word usage, etc.) + 150 TF-IDF SVD components = 192 total features
